In the early days, essentially, I'll try not to go too deep in the history where it's less relevant, but we were signing three-month contracts.
They were rolling three-month contracts.
And so it was basically like you sign up for initial three months.
You can give us 30 days notice in any period of time.
And then a lot of our initial agreements were set like that.
That was good for closing companies early on.
was relatively like low risk.
But to like really like make ourselves more appealing because we really started to see more opportunity for us to grow faster and then to fundraise, we really wanted to lock in 12-month agreements.
But the challenge of especially that, keep in mind like where we were starting to have this conversation was about a year ago, actually.
And so Growthex started in, you know, somewhere in the range.
We should have like an actual date, like.
I'm not sure Marcel even has it in his mind, like April, June-ish of 2024, then like started to really ramp up around like August, September.
And so like we're still relatively like young company and I mean we still are, right?
So getting people to sign 12-month agreements was like, you know, not the easiest thing to do, right?
And so what we did was we kind of introduced this like pilot period where, what did we, I'm remember now.
I think it was, yeah, it was a 90-day pilot in the beginning of the 12-month agreement, but we would ask for 30 days notice.

So in effect, it was really 60 days because it was like, you can sign up, you get a feel for the relationship, you know, how, you know, how confident are you going to be signing up for that 12 months?
And so we've made it low risk for people to sign up, but we were finding a...
It was almost like too frictionless.
And so we were not being as selective as we probably should have been.
And nor were they being as maybe thorough and thinking about the engagement as they should have been.
And so we were finding like around after some really, really rapid growth around like April-ish, March-April range, like a decent amount of like opt-outs happening.
And some of that was like, okay, like similar, maybe we don't want to work with these people.
But like there was definitely a lot more than was ideal.
And there was a whole bunch of like part of the hypothesis was what I just mentioned.
But an additional part was just like, how can we ensure we actually lay the right foundation early on to like to move fast, but also move fast on the right things.
And what we were finding is that we were moving really, really fast.
But sometimes...
sometimes...
Not getting enough context to really set the right strategy.

It was almost like because speed was our superpower to like getting a lot of content out there, the way it was being sold and pitched, it was almost like they expected content so fast and so much of it and then results so fast.
And it's just like, okay, that's not how it works.
That's not like the most ideal way of thinking about this.
And so there's a lot of different layers to this.
And so we had, we decided to come up with this strategy sprint process that was really driven by Daniel and it was a pretty radical change in some ways.
It was still structured around a 90 day-ish kind of, or no, in this case, eight weeks, sorry.
But it was more around like, let's actually clearly define what happens in this period of time.
And there is a mutual opt-out.

Out opportunity towards the end of this period and that we're going to really start to say, still doing some of the things that we wanted to do, but more clearly structuring it and getting to a point of like, what does that exit criteria look like for when we feel confident that they are ready to truly be in a place where we can move fast on the right things.
And so, again, some of that is like talking to the right people to get that right context and developing those artifacts.
What we introduced more consistently was going deeper with their product and technical teams, if applicable, going beyond just a simple kickoff, for example.
Like, how do you really have like those deep dive conversations to appreciate their customers, the product at a deeper level?
Like, what are we actually doing this for and getting that context?
Um, starting to then from those after aligning on those artifacts, like really start to make sure we're dialing in.

The strategy, as we have that context of the artifacts, all of their company, the pain points of their customers that they solve for, et cetera, how does that relate to the strategy, align with them on that strategy, then we start to execute upon it.
This is, again, stuff that we would do in the past, but just in a more structured and comprehensive way.

And the idea is that if we do that right in those right steps and we start to execute upon it, that we should start to have rich learnings through that back and forth, that calibration process of what does it actually mean for us to this strategy that we all agreed on?
What does it mean for us to actually execute on it?
And what does your feedback look like?
Who's involved in that feedback?
How many rounds are we finding that it takes for us to get to this point?
What do we learn from that feedback that we feel like we can address and make the next round all the much easier?
And so if we go through enough of that, then...

And by the time it gets to the delivery team post-Sprint, we should be in this place where we can hand them this system in a sense of like, here's the plan, here are the key players, here's all the context.
This is, you know, this is what we need to do over the next handful of months.
It should far better set up that team for success, where in the past they were trying to do those things with some new accounts and then some existing accounts.

And like kind of being pulled multiple directions, whereas separating these teams enable us to focus on those critical like parts of the life cycle of that engagement to just be more consistent and get these accounts to a healthier spot.
So largely it's been a very successful change for us.
